text: The laparoscopic view shows the gallbladder, the common hepatic duct, and the cystic duct. The common hepatic duct is visible leading into the common bile duct, with the gallbladder positioned nearby.

The surgeon continues blunt dissection using a suction irrigator to identify anatomical structures around the gallbladder. Once the anatomy is clearly identified, the surgeon prepares to dissect using a Maryland dissector.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and begins dissection to free it from the infundibulum of the gallbladder. The remnant of the cystic artery is visible overlying the cystic duct, and dissection is performed around it using a Maryland dissector.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully dissect at the anticipated junction between the infundibulum of the gallbladder and the cystic duct, starting laterally and progressing medially.

The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to dissect along the sides of the gallbladder, freeing it from the liver's edge. The dissection begins at the infundibulum and progresses towards the liver, carefully avoiding the liver bed and cystic plate while maintaining the junction between the gallbladder and liver.

The surgical field shows dense adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding tissues due to chronic inflammation. The surgeon attempts to free the gallbladder from the liver to obtain a critical view, but the cystic artery remains obscured and difficult to identify.

The surgeon divides the remnant of the cystic artery using electrocautery and continues the dissection around the cystic duct. Due to significant inflammation, the common bile duct and common hepatic duct are visible in close proximity, requiring careful dissection to avoid injury. The dissection proceeds from medial to lateral and vice versa, following the natural tissue planes.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create space around the cystic duct for clip placement. Three metallic clips are placed on the stay side and one on the specimen side, or two locking clips on the stay side and one on the specimen side. Endoshears are then used to sharply divide the cystic duct.

With the cystic duct divided, the surgeon observes the gallbladder densely adhered to surrounding structures and the cystic plate. The hepatic duct is visible in close proximity, necessitating careful and methodical dissection to avoid injury.

The surgeon uses gentle bursts of electrocautery to dissect the tissue, revealing an area of dense adhesion. A Maryland dissector is employed to carefully tease out the tissue, which is expected to be loose areolar tissue but appears inflamed due to a previously perforated gallbladder.

The surgeon performs gentle and methodical dissection around the gallbladder, carefully navigating the inflamed and densely adhered tissues. Due to the challenging nature of the case, the surgeon considers a subtotal cholecystectomy, leaving the posterior wall of the gallbladder intact if necessary.

The surgeon continues gentle dissection, progressively breaking apart dense tissue adhesions around the gallbladder. Despite the tedious nature of the procedure, the operation remains safe, with visible progress in separating the gallbladder from surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects around a structure initially identified as unusual, suggesting it may be a variant of normal anatomy, ensuring the operation proceeds safely without injuring major structures.

The surgeon identifies a tubular structure, potentially an accessory duct off the right hepatic duct, and clips it on both the stay and specimen sides. Using endoshears, the duct is divided between the clips. The aberrant duct is dissected around using a Maryland dissector before division.

The surgeon continues careful dissection to release the gallbladder from the liver bed, using a suction irrigator as a blunt dissector to gently push around tissues without applying excessive pressure.

The surgeon uses electrocautery in gentle, small bursts to further mobilize the gallbladder, avoiding injury to surrounding structures. The gallbladder is nearly fully mobilized, but a clear areolar plane is not yet identified. Each burst of energy frees more of the gallbladder, with caution taken to prevent entry into the liver bed.

The surgeon has inadvertently entered the gallbladder, revealing a large, calcified stone embedded in the surrounding tissue. Bleeding is observed from the liver bed, prompting careful use of electrocautery to manage it. The surgeon dissects along the posterior wall of the gallbladder, intentionally leaving it intact to avoid further entry into the liver bed.

The surgeon uses gentle bursts of electrocautery and counter traction to carefully remove the gallbladder from the liver bed, leaving only a small piece of the posterior wall intact. The gallbladder is mostly detached, and the liver bed is coagulated to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon places the fully removed gallbladder into an endo-catch bag and irrigates the gallbladder fossa. The clips on the ducts are visualized and confirmed to be intact, with no injury to surrounding structures. Attention is then directed to a large gallstone residing in the mesentery, indicating a previous gallbladder perforation. The surgeon prepares to remove the encapsulated gallstone from the abdomen.

The surgeon applies gentle bursts of electrocautery on the anterior aspect, revealing a capsule with no posterior tissues visible. The stone is placed in an endo-catch bag for removal from the patient's abdomen, completing the operation.
